title: Consent Motion to Change
subtitle: Ontario Family Court — Family Law Rules (FLR 15)
helpIntro: Form 23 is used when both parties agree to change an existing court order — for example, changing a support amount, a parenting schedule, or another term — without having to go to a full court hearing. Both parties must sign this form. If one party does not agree, you must use Form 14 (Notice of Motion) instead.

parts.title: Court information
parts.subtitle: Step 1 of 6
parts.intro: Enter the court file information from the existing order you want to change.

fields.label: Courthouse
options: St. Catharines — Superior Court of Justice
Thunder Bay — Superior Court of Justice

fields.label: Court file number (from the existing order)
fields.helpText: This is printed on the court order you want to change.

fields.label: Date of the existing order you want to change
fields.helpText: The date printed at the top or bottom of the order.

fields.label: Name of the judge or officer who made the existing order (if known)

parts.title: The parties
parts.subtitle: Step 2 of 6
parts.intro: Confirm both parties' names and contact information.

fields.label: Applicant's full legal name
Applicant's address for service
City
Postal code
Phone number
Respondent's full legal name
Respondent's address for service
Respondent's phone number

parts.title: What you want to change
parts.subtitle: Step 3 of 6
parts.intro: Describe exactly what terms of the existing order you both agree to change. Be specific — the judge will make the new order based on exactly what you write here.

fields.label: Are you changing child support?
Current child support amount in the existing order
New child support amount you both agree to
Start date for the new child support amount
fields.helpText: Usually the first of the month following the agreed change.

fields.label: Why are you changing child support?
fields.placeholder: e.g. The payor's income has changed. The payor now earns $X per year. The new amount reflects the applicable Child Support Guidelines table amount.
fields.helpText: The court needs to know there has been a material change in circumstances.

fields.label: Are you changing spousal support?
Current spousal support amount in the existing order
New spousal support amount you both agree to
fields.helpText: Enter 0 if you are agreeing to terminate spousal support.

fields.label: Start date for the new spousal support amount
Why are you changing spousal support?
fields.placeholder: e.g. The recipient has become self-sufficient and both parties agree support should end.

fields.label: Are you changing parenting time or decision-making?
Current parenting arrangement in the existing order
fields.placeholder: e.g. Currently: children live primarily with Applicant, Respondent has parenting time every other weekend.

fields.label: New parenting arrangement you both agree to
fields.placeholder: e.g. New arrangement: children alternate weekly between both homes (week-on, week-off). Each parent to have the children on their respective weeks from Sunday at 6:00 p.m. to the following Sunday at 6:00 p.m.
fields.helpText: Be as specific as possible — include days, times, holiday schedules, and any special provisions.

fields.label: Why are you changing the parenting arrangement?
fields.placeholder: e.g. The children are older and both parties agree a week-on, week-off schedule better meets the children's current needs and schedules.

fields.label: Are you changing any other terms of the existing order?
Describe the other changes you both agree to
fields.placeholder: e.g. Paragraph 4 of the existing order regarding the family pet is deleted. The parties agree that the dog 'Max' shall reside primarily with the Applicant.

parts.title: Children (if applicable)
parts.subtitle: Step 4 of 6
parts.intro: If the changes affect children, provide their details.

fields.label: Do the changes involve children?
List the children (name and date of birth)
fields.placeholder:
Alex Lance — born April 12, 2015
Sam Lance — born September 3, 2018

fields.label: Why are the proposed changes in the best interests of the children?
fields.placeholder: e.g. Both parties agree that the children are old enough to benefit from equal time with each parent. Both parents live within the same school district so the children's routines will not be disrupted.

parts.title: Consent of both parties
parts.subtitle: Step 5 of 6
parts.intro: Both parties must consent to the changes. This section confirms that both of you agree freely and without pressure.

fields.label: I (the applicant) freely consent to the changes described in this form.
Date of applicant's consent
I confirm the respondent has also agreed to these changes and will sign this form.
fields.helpText: The respondent must physically sign the printed form before it is filed. Both signatures are required.

fields.label: Have both parties had an opportunity to get legal advice before signing?
fields.helpText: You are not required to have a lawyer, but the court will want to know you both understood what you were agreeing to.

parts.title: Review and finalize
parts.subtitle: Step 6 of 6
parts.intro: Review the consent motion. Once both parties sign the printed version, file it with the court clerk. You do not need a hearing — the clerk will submit it to a judge for approval.

fields.label: When should the new order take effect?
fields.helpText: Leave blank if you want it to take effect immediately upon the court's approval.

fields.label: Agreement on costs
options: No order as to costs — each party pays their own
Costs to be determined by the court

fields.label: Any additional terms both parties agree to include
fields.placeholder: e.g. The parties shall communicate regarding the children via the OurFamilyWizard app only.

fields.label: I confirm this Consent Motion accurately reflects the agreement of both parties and I am signing freely and voluntarily.

description: Used when both parties agree to change an existing court order and want to do so by consent, without a hearing. Must be signed by both parties or their lawyers. Used for changes to child support, spousal support, or parenting arrangements.
